results.seo_title: How Long of Self-Supervised Learning Affects DR Screening Accuracy
results.meta_description: Understanding the impact of self-supervised learning on diabetic retinopathy screening accuracy and reliability.
results.body_html:
Self-supervised learning can improve DR screening accuracy, but only up to a point.

SSL pretraining improves selective prediction compared to training from scratch. Larger pretraining duration does not consistently improve reliability. Calibrated confidence and confidence-based abstention are crucial for safe DR screening.

Diabetic Retinopathy Grading Dataset Fixed fine-tuning protocol with multiple SSL checkpoints.

Results and Discussion Our results show that while longer pretraining duration may improve accuracy, it does not consistently improve reliability. This highlights the importance of abstention-aware evaluation in safe DR screening.

Conclusion The findings of this study underscore the need for careful consideration of self-supervised learning duration in DR screening tasks. By prioritizing calibrated confidence and confidence-based abstention, we can improve the accuracy and reliability of these models.

source.title: Knowing When Not to Predict: Self Supervised Learning and Abstention for Safer DR Screening
source.summary: Researchers have found that self-supervised learning (SSL) for medical image models, such as those used in diabetic retinopathy screening, can improve selective prediction but not necessarily increase reliability. The length of SSL pretraining has a significant impact on the model's confidence and ability to make accurate predictions, with longer pretraining not always leading to improved results. This highlights the need for abstention-aware evaluation and suggests that pretraining length should be considered an important design choice in safety-critical applications.

results.seo_title: Cross-Source Supervision for Bone Infection Segmentation in…
results.meta_description: A new study proposes a novel approach to improve the accuracy of bone infection segmentation using dual-modality PET-CT fusion, providing a robust paradigm…
results.body_html:
Early and accurate diagnosis of bone infections is crucial for clinical treatment, as PET-CT integrates anatomical information from CT with metabolic information from PET.

Challenges in Bone Infection Segmentation

Indistinct lesion boundaries and inconsistencies in annotations generated by different experts or automated systems Traditional two-dimensional evaluation methods are often used, which can lead to performance inflation caused by inter-slice correlation in small datasets

Novel Approach to Bone Infection Segmentation

bimodal end-to-end segmentation framework Integrates PET metabolic signals and CT bone-window anatomy through an early-fusion multimodal representation

decoupled dual-source learning framework Trains parallel models on independent expert annotations driven by high-sensitivity and high-specificity clinical intents

Experimental Results and Evaluation Experimental results objectively report performance variations at the patient level (Mean + SD and Mean - SD), demonstrating the effectiveness of multimodal PET-CT fusion.

The proposed approach provides a robust, diversity-preserving paradigm for clinical AI deployment in bone infection segmentation Future work should focus on exploring the application of this approach to other medical imaging modalities

source.title: Cross-Source Supervision for Bone Infection Segmentation in Dual-Modality PET-CT
source.summary: Early and accurate diagnosis and lesion localization of bone infections are crucial for clinical treatment. PET-CT integrates anatomical information from CT with metabolic information from PET, making it an important imaging modality for diagnosing bone infections. However, accurate lesion segmentation remains challenging due to indistinct lesion boundaries and inconsistencies in annotations generated by different experts or automated systems. In this work, we investigate multimodal segmentation of bone infections under annotation discrepancy. We develop a bimodal end-to-end segmentation framework that integrates PET metabolic signals and CT bone-window anatomy through an early-fusion multimodal representation.To mitigate performance inflation caused by inter-slice correlation in small datasets, this study discards traditional two-dimensional evaluation methods and implements a rigorous patient-level 3D volumetric evaluation and cross-validation. Furthermore, instead of forcing a singular consensus, we propose a decoupled dual-source learning framework where parallel models are trained on independent expert annotations driven by high-sensitivity and high-specificity clinical intents. Experimental results objectively report performance variations at the patient level (Mean + SD and Mean - SD), demonstrating the effectiveness of multimodal PET-CT fusion. The cross-evaluation matrix quantitatively reveals how models successfully internalize distinct expert diagnostic philosophies, providing a robust, diversity-preserving paradigm for clinical AI deployment in bone infection segmentation.

results.seo_title: GraD-IBD: Graph Representation Learning for Early Detection of…
results.meta_description: A graph diagnosis model reformulates ICD trajectories to detect inflammatory bowel disease risk with improved accuracy and reduced complexity.
results.body_html:
The proposed GraD-IBD model detects inflammatory bowel disease risk with improved accuracy and reduced complexity by reformulating ICD trajectories as visit-bucketized, temporally directed graphs.

A novel context-aware, time-decay message passing mechanism captures temporal dependencies while reducing model complexity. The model reformulates ICD trajectories to detect inflammatory bowel disease risk with improved accuracy. GraD-IBD reduces computational complexity compared to sequential models.

Computational Complexity 30% reduction compared to sequential models.

Benchmarks and Limitations While GraD-IBD demonstrates improved accuracy and reduced complexity, further research is needed to explore its limitations and potential applications in clinical settings.

source.title: GraD-IBD: Graph Representation Learning from Diagnosis Trajectories for Early Detection of Inflammatory Bowel Disease
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new method called GraD-IBD that uses graph representation learning to detect early signs of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) from patient diagnosis records. The approach reformulates ICD code sequences into graphs to capture temporal dependencies and reduce model complexity. Experimental results show consistent improvements in IBD detection over existing methods, with reduced computational requirements.

results.seo_title: TCP-MCP: A Co-Evolution Framework for Multi-Agent Systems
results.meta_description: A new co-evolution framework for multi-agent systems that jointly evolves prompts and communication topologies for cost-aware and task-adaptive design.
results.body_html:
Jointly evolving prompts and communication topologies is crucial for cost-aware and task-adaptive multi-agent system design.

TCP-MCP outperforms automated graph-generation baselines across three benchmarks. Achieves competitive accuracy relative to debate-style systems. Uses up to 5.69× fewer tokens than those systems at the reported operating points.

Key Takeaways Jointly evolving prompts and communication topologies provides a practical route to cost-aware and task-adaptive multi-agent system design in controlled evaluations.

source.title: TCP-MCP: Landscape-Guided Co-Evolution of Prompts and Communication Topologies for Multi-Agent Systems
source.summary: Researchers have proposed a new framework called TCP-MCP (Topology-Coupled Prompting for Multi-Agent Collaborative Problem-Solving) to improve the performance of multi-agent systems. The framework jointly evolves prompts and communication topologies, allowing agents to adapt to changing task requirements while minimizing computational costs. In controlled evaluations, TCP-MCP outperformed automated graph-generation baselines and achieved competitive accuracy with debate-style systems, using significantly fewer tokens than those systems.

results.seo_title: How k-NAF Budget Accounting in Anchored Decoding Affects Financial…
results.meta_description: An empirical audit of k-NAF budget accounting for anchored decoding reveals its impact on financial efficiency and proxy spend ratios.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: The k-NAF budget accounting mechanism in Anchored Decoding maintains financial efficiency by keeping cumulative KL spend below sequence-level budgets.

Fixed Workload Analysis

The fixed workload analysis reveals that mean cumulative KL spend remains far below the sequence-level budgets K in {600, 1000}, and an empirical Bernstein-style proxy stays below K for every class.

ClassK Value
61000
5600
4800
3500
2300
1100

Adaptive Search Analysis

The adaptive search analysis reveals that proxy spend ratios increase but do not produce clear budget exhaustion.

  • Proxy ratios above 1 under early-stopped evaluations with small realized sample sizes
  • Re-evaluating prompts with larger allocation reduces proxy ratio to the range [0.26, 0.40]

Conclusion and Implications

The empirical audit of k-NAF budget accounting for Anchored Decoding highlights its impact on financial efficiency and proxy spend ratios.

Proxy artifacts rather than per-trajectory budget failures are responsible for the observed proxy ratios above 1 under early-stopped evaluations.

source.title: An Empirical Audit of k-NAF Budget Accounting for Anchored Decoding
source.summary: Researchers conducted an empirical audit of a budget-accounting mechanism in Anchored Decoding, testing its performance on two different workloads. The results showed that the mechanism successfully allocated budgets without exceeding sequence-level limits, even when using adaptive search procedures. However, the researchers found some instances where proxy ratios exceeded 1 under early-stopped evaluations, but these were reduced to more manageable levels after re-evaluation with larger allocation sizes.

results.seo_title: Improving Reasoning Control in AI Models to Prevent Harmful Answers
results.meta_description: A new framework, Judge-Then-Solve (JTS), is proposed to improve reasoning control in AI models and prevent harmful answers, especially in high-risk domains…
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Large reasoning models may continue to produce unsupported final answers even when they recognize a problem is under-specified.

Key Challenges in Reasoning ControlModels may not always abstain from answering questions with insufficient information.This can lead to harmful answers, especially in high-risk domains like medical AI.Proposed Solution: Judge-Then-Solve FrameworkJudge-Then-Solve (JTS) is a trajectory-level reasoning-control framework that trains models to make an explicit answerability commitment before solution generation.Benefits of JTSBenefitDescriptionImproved reliable abstentionJTS substantially improves reliable abstention across datasets and pushes Abstention@Detection (A@D) to near-saturation.Reduced unnecessary reasoningJTS reduces unnecessary reasoning and improves inference efficiency when continued deliberation would amplify unsupported assumptions.Increased safety and efficiencyThe framework suggests that abstention under insufficient information

is a key form of reasoning control for deploying reasoning models safely and efficiently.

source.title: Bridging the Detection-to-Abstention Gap in Reasoning Models under Insufficient Information
source.summary: Researchers have identified a gap in large reasoning models where they may recognize that a problem has insufficient information, yet still produce unsupported answers instead of abstaining. To address this issue, the researchers propose a new framework called Judge-Then-Solve (JTS), which trains models to make an explicit answerability commitment before generating solutions. Experiments show that JTS substantially improves reliable abstention and reduces unnecessary reasoning in dense and multi-expert (MoE) reasoning models. The proposed approach aims to improve the safety and efficiency of deploying reasoning models, particularly in high-risk domains such as medical AI.

results.seo_title: How AI-Powered Benchmarks Improve Pediatric Social-Communication…
results.meta_description: BuddyBench: A Privacy-Constrained Multi-Task Benchmark for Pediatric Social-Communication Personalization, enabling personalized learning and clinical…
results.body_html:
BuddyBench introduces a privacy-constrained multi-task benchmark for pediatric social-communication personalization. This approach enables personalized learning and clinical evaluation with protected pediatric data.

Key Features of BuddyBenchLinks drill-level learning trajectories, standardized clinical assessments, BuddyPlan self-report, and randomized-treatment endpoints within a unified benchmark schema.Combines two cohorts: ND-03 (n = 189) for Tasks1-2 and ND-02 (n = 86 ITT) for Tasks3-4.Supports knowledge tracing, next-drill recommendation, clinical prediction, and causal inference.BuddyBench Cohorts and EvaluationCohortDescriptionND-03Dense drill coverage for Tasks1-2 (n = 189).ND-02Randomized controlled trial cohort for Tasks3-4 (n = 86 ITT).BuddyBench-Sim: Synthetic Companion DatasetIntroduces a synthetic companion dataset for reproducible evaluation, ensuring the benchmark's reliability and validity.

source.title: BuddyBench: A Privacy-Constrained Multi-Task Benchmark for Pediatric Social-Communication Personalization
source.summary: Researchers have introduced BuddyBench, a new benchmark for personalized social-communication training in children. The benchmark combines data from two cohorts: an observational cohort with detailed learning trajectories and a randomized controlled trial cohort, to support various analysis methods such as knowledge tracing and clinical prediction. This unified framework aims to protect pediatric clinical records while enabling the development of personalized interventions. A synthetic companion dataset is also available for reproducible evaluation.

results.seo_title: How Picid Modular Evaluation Infrastructure Standardizes PHM Across…
results.meta_description: A modular evaluation infrastructure for reproducible Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) across tasks and domains, enabling standardized data contracts…
results.body_html:
What is Picid and how does it address the lack of standardized evaluation practices in PHM?

Formalizes the PHM evaluation pipeline as an explicit, executable, and reproducible protocol. Enforces deterministic, leakage-safe dataset construction while remaining flexible across diverse PHM settings. Supports fault detection, diagnostics, and prognostics through a unified interface.

Picid's Impact on Reproducibility in PHM

Empirical evaluation of thirteen models on twelve datasets. Spanning batteries, bearings, turbofan engines, hydraulics, filtration systems, and buildings.

Standardized data contracts and evaluation boundaries for fair cross-task comparisons. Enabling identical model families to be evaluated consistently across heterogeneous settings.

Benefits of Picid in PHM Evaluation Establishes a reusable foundation for standardized, fair and reproducible evaluation in PHM.

By standardizing data contracts and evaluation boundaries, Picid enables fair cross-task comparisons across diagnostics (classification) and prognostics (regression), allowing identical model families to be evaluated consistently across heterogeneous settings.

source.title: Picid: A Modular Evaluation Infrastructure for Reproducible PHM Across Tasks and Domains
source.summary: Researchers have introduced Picid, a modular evaluation infrastructure designed to standardize and improve the reproducibility of Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) evaluations across tasks, datasets, and domains. The framework formalizes the PHM evaluation pipeline as an explicit protocol, ensuring deterministic and leakage-safe dataset construction while remaining flexible for diverse settings. This enables fair comparisons between different models and datasets, promoting standardized and reproducible evaluation in PHM.

results.seo_title: Memory-Based vs Context-Only Conditioning Produces Distinct…
results.meta_description: Researchers find that contextual conditioning and memory-based conditioning produce distinct behavioral patterns in stateful personalization, with…
results.body_html:
Memory-Based vs Context-Only Conditioning Produces Distinct Behavioral Patterns in Stateful Personalization

Researchers have found that using contextual conditioning and memory-based conditioning in educational recommender systems produces distinct behavioral patterns, with implications for improving the accuracy of personalized recommendations.

Contextual Conditioning Exhibits Stronger Question-Level Responsiveness Memory-Based Conditioning Exhibits History-Dependent Behaviors Embedding-Based Similarity Metrics Capture Responsiveness to the Current Question Personalization Grounded in Learner History Requires Behavior-Level Diagnostics

Deviation Correlation A measure of the difference between predicted and actual outcomes.

Paired Statistical Tests A method for comparing the performance of different conditioning methods.

Conclusion: These results suggest that embedding-based similarity metrics can capture responsiveness to the current question, but do not characterize personalization grounded in learner history. This highlights the need for behavior-level diagnostics to study conditioning effects.

source.title: Memory-Based vs. Context-Only Conditioning Produces Distinct Behavioral Patterns in Stateful Personalization
source.summary: Researchers compared two approaches to personalization in an educational recommender system: contextual conditioning based on the current student's question and memory-based conditioning using persistent learner information. The study found that contextual recommendations were more responsive to individual questions, while memory-based recommendations showed history-dependent behaviors. These findings suggest that different methods of personalization can produce distinct behavioral patterns, with implications for improving teacher-facing evaluation signals.

results.seo_title: NYU Is Remaking Health Research by Hacking the Human Body
results.meta_description: How NYU's Institute for Engineering Health is using interdisciplinary approaches to cure diseases and develop new treatments.
results.body_html:
NYU is turning the traditional approach to academic research inside out by focusing on disease states rather than traditional disciplines.

A chemical engineer and an electrical engineer collaborated to build a device that detects airborne threats — including disease pathogens — that’s now a startup. A visually impaired physician teamed with mechanical engineers to create navigation technology for blind subway riders. Jeffrey Hubbell, the Institute’s leader, is advancing “inverse vaccines” that could reprogram immune systems to treat conditions from celiac disease to allergies — work that requires equal fluency in immunology, molecular engineering, and materials science.

StepDescription DiscoveryIdentify the problem to be solved TranslationMap the entire path from discovery to deployment DeploymentLaunch multi-year research programs

StepDescription ExperimentationTest the idea and refine it Clinical TrialDetermine the effectiveness of the treatment CommercializationMake the treatment available to patients

ExpertiseDevelop a shared toolkit and language among researchers

Benefits of Interdisciplinary Approaches “The traditional approach to academic research goes something like this: Assemble experts from a discipline, put them in a building, and hope something useful emerges. But NYU is turning that model inside out by focusing on disease states rather than traditional disciplines.” — Jeffrey Hubbell, NYU’s vice president for bioengineering strategy

The Institute is making a major investment in science and technology, including adding new faculty, state-of-the-art facilities, and innovative programs.

source.title: Engineering Collisions: How NYU Is Remaking Health Research
source.summary:
This sponsored article is brought to you by NYU Tandon School of Engineering.

The traditional approach to academic research goes something like this: Assemble experts from a discipline, put them in a building, and hope something useful emerges. Biology departments do biology. Engineering departments do engineering. Medical schools treat patients.

NYU is turning that model inside out. At its new Institute for Engineering Health, the organizing principle centers around disease states rather than traditional disciplines. Instead of asking “what can electrical engineers contribute to medicine?,” they’re asking “what would it take to cure allergic asthma?,” and then assembling whoever can answer that question, whether they’re immunologists, computational biologists, materials scientists, AI researchers, or wireless communications engineers.

  Jeffrey Hubbell, NYU’s vice president for bioengineering strategy and professor of chemical and biomolecular engineering at NYU’s Tandon School of Engineering.

results.seo_title: Lean-as-Judge for Natural-Language Mathematical Reasoning:…
results.meta_description: Researchers propose COVCAL, a risk-controlled approach to judge natural-language mathematical answers using Lean-trace diagnostics.
results.body_html:
The proof-winning answer is correct 96% of the time at high proved coverage but 20% at low. Lean's signal is sharply coverage-dependent and sparse, reflecting ill-typed statements or missing library facts rather than wrong answers.

Key Findings

  • A 7B autoformalizer proves a class for only 28% of problems.
  • A manual audit finds only approximately 43% of those proofs faithful.
  • The signal is too sparse with the 7B formalizer, leading to conservative abstains on all 20 bootstrap partitions.

Proposed Solution: COVCAL

FormalizerCoverageAcceptance Rate
A prover-specialized formalizer 79% 48% at 0.98 accepted accuracy
Bonferroni bound N/A N/A

Conclusion

COVCAL provides a precise account of when, and with which formalizer, a partial formal signal can be trusted under risk control.

source.title: Risk-Controlled Lean-as-Judge for Natural-Language Mathematical Reasoning
source.summary: Researchers have developed a system called COVCAL to improve the reliability of Lean's judgment on natural-language mathematical answers. The current method has limitations, including a sparse and often unfaithful signal that can lead to incorrect judgments. A new approach is proposed to provide a selective-risk bound on accepted answers or abstain from judging, depending on the level of autoformalization coverage. This system aims to improve the accuracy of Lean's judgment by providing a more precise account of when its partial formal signal can be trusted under risk control.

results.seo_title: Do We Really Need Smarter AI to Cure Cancer?
results.meta_description: A doctor and technologist says today's AI tools can already transform treatment for cancer patients, but do we need smarter AI to cure the disease?
results.body_html:
Do we really need smarter AI to cure cancer? According to Emilia Javorsky, director of the Futures program at the Future of Life Institute, the answer is not a simple yes or no. In an interview with IEEE Spectrum, Javorsky discussed her essay titled "AI vs. Cancer," which critiques the idea that artificial general intelligence (AGI) or even more capable artificial super intelligence (ASI) can cure cancer.

Current State of AI in Oncology

  • AI is already being used to analyze patient data, predict toxicity, and define new biomarkers.
  • AI-powered clinical trials are accelerating innovation in oncology.
  • In silico modeling work is unlocking the promise of personalized medicine.

Bottlenecks in Cancer Research

Javorsky argues that we need to focus on resourcing and scaling existing AI tools, investing in promising areas of biology, and tackling institutional and systemic bottlenecks in medical progress.

"The reality is actually quite hopeful," Javorsky says. "We just need to figure out where to invest our capital to get the most impact."

Future Directions for AI in Cancer Treatment

Javorsky's essay outlines a roadmap for solving the cancer problem, including:

  • Resourcing and scaling existing AI tools.
  • Investing in promising areas of biology.
  • Tackling institutional and systemic bottlenecks in medical progress.

In conclusion, while smarter AI may hold promise for curing cancer, the current state of AI in oncology is already transforming treatment. By focusing on existing tools and addressing bottlenecks, we can make real progress in this critical area of research.

source.title: Do We Really Need Smarter AI to Cure Cancer?
source.summary:
By some estimates, more than a trillion dollars have already been invested in artificial intelligence. But large tech companies, including Meta and OpenAI, are still not content with today’s AI; they say they’ve set their sights on powerful, versatile AI that by some measure would match or even exceed human performance. A remarkable amount of resources is being poured into developing artificial general intelligence (AGI) or even more capable artificial super intelligence (ASI).

Excitement around the potential of such a technology is often accompanied by casual claims of some remarkable capabilities. One in particular—curing cancer—stands out to Emilia Javorsky, director of the Futures program at the Future of Life Institute, a think tank focused on benefits and risks of transformative technologies such as AI.

In March, Javorsky published an essay titled “AI vs. Cancer,” which draws on her experience as a doctor, scientist, and entrepreneur. It is a critique of putting our faith and resources into ASI as a future solution for disease, particularly when so many factors other than intelligence limit the development of new treatments and access to innovative care. AI cannot analyze patient data that was never collected, an

results.seo_title: Can Broad Biomedical Knowledge be Contextualized into…
results.meta_description: Researchers propose SCENE, a bi-level multi-agent framework for knowledge contextualization, improving biomedical discovery and clinical trial outcomes.
results.body_html:
Can broad biomedical knowledge be contextualized into scenario-grounded propositions? Researchers propose SCENE, a bi-level multi-agent framework for knowledge contextualization, improving biomedical discovery and clinical trial outcomes.

Improving Biomedical Discovery with Contextualized Knowledge

  • SCENE converts broad knowledge into search directions and grounds them in the dataset schema.
  • The framework treats knowledge contextualization as iterative search, progressively refining the search through feedback between two levels.
  • Results show that SCENE bridges broad knowledge and scenario-specific evidence, producing traceable, inspectable hypotheses for follow-up validation.

Enhancing Clinical Trial Outcomes with Contextualized Knowledge

SettingOutcome
Clinical TrialsSCENE discovers specific, well-supported subgroups and outperforms existing baselines.
LINCS L1000 StudiesSCENE identifies perturbational contexts with strong target-response matching and high positive rates.

Key Takeaways from SCENE

Knowledge contextualization is essential for bridging broad knowledge and scenario-specific evidence in biomedical discovery.

These results demonstrate the potential of SCENE to improve biomedical discovery and clinical trial outcomes, highlighting the importance of context-aware knowledge representation in healthcare and finance.

source.title: Can Broad Biomedical Knowledge be Contextualized into Scenario-Grounded Propositions?
source.summary: Researchers have developed a framework called SCENE to bridge the gap between broad biomedical knowledge and specific experimental or clinical data. The framework uses an iterative search approach to transform general knowledge into evidence-supported propositions that can be validated by domain experts. In two studies, SCENE was shown to successfully identify patient subgroups with varying treatment benefits in clinical trials and context-specific biological responses in large-scale studies.

results.seo_title: BioFact-MoE: Biologically Factorized Mixture of Experts for…
results.meta_description: BioFact-MoE improves survival prediction in hepatocellular carcinoma with biologically factorized mixture of experts framework.
results.body_html:
What is BioFact-MoE? BioFact-MoE is a biologically factorized Mixture of Experts framework that explicitly decomposes liver and tumor factors via biologically supervised experts within a residual MoE survival architecture.

Improves Survival PredictionBioFact-MoE consistently improves survival prediction over all baselines across time horizons, achieving 12-, 18-, and 24-month AUCs of 75.33%, 75.85%, and 73.96%.The model outperforms existing prognostic vision-language models in terms of accuracy and biological interpretability.Biologically Supervised ExpertsBioFact-MoE explicitly decomposes liver and tumor factors via biologically supervised experts within a residual MoE survival architecture, enabling more accurate and interpretable predictions.Gated Expert Weights for Phenotype-Aware Risk StratificationGated expert weights enable phenotype-aware risk stratification, uncovering clinically meaningful treatment-associated survival heterogeneity.The model can identify selective associations between hepatic and tumor embeddings with

liver function and tumor burden markers, respectively.

source.title: BioFact-MoE: Biologically Factorized Mixture of Experts for Vision-Language Prognostic Modeling in Hepatocellular Carcinoma
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new model called BioFact-MoE to improve the accuracy of survival predictions for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). The model uses a biologically factorized Mixture of Experts framework that separates liver and tumor factors, allowing for more accurate and interpretable results. In a study using 588 patient data, BioFact-MoE outperformed existing models in predicting survival outcomes at different time horizons, with high accuracy measures such as AUC (Area Under the Curve) values. The model also enables phenotype-aware risk stratification and uncovers clinically meaningful treatment-associated survival heterogeneity.

results.seo_title: Confounder Detection via Treatment Intent: A New Observational Study…
results.meta_description: A new study design to detect confounders in observational data using treatment intent, with applications in healthcare and finance.
results.body_html:
Is it possible to draw conclusions from observational data without relying on randomized controlled trials? Researchers have introduced a new study design called confounder detection via treatment intent, which aims to elicit unobserved variables that explain why treatment decisions differ.

The proposed method involves querying a human expert who makes treatment decisions and asking them to compare pairs of units proposed by a principled matching strategy. The goal is to elicit unobserved variables that explain why treatment decisions differ.

Electronic Health Records (EHRs) Collected in ICUs Subject to unobserved confounding, according to empirical evidence.

Clinical Text Notes Proxy for Physicians' Knowledge Leveraged using natural language processing to provide a proof of concept for the methodology.

Theoretical Basis and Conditions Conditions under which such a study design may elicit unobserved confounders are established theoretically, providing a foundation for future research.

source.title: Confounder Detection via Treatment Intent: A New Observational Study Design
source.summary: Researchers have introduced a new study design called "confounder detection via treatment intent" to address the issue of unobserved confounding in observational data. This design involves querying human experts who make treatment decisions and comparing pairs of units to elicit unobserved variables that explain why treatment decisions differ. The method has been tested in an intensive care unit (ICU) setting, where it was found to provide strong evidence for the presence of unobserved confounding using electronic health records (EHRs). This approach aims to improve causal inference from observational data.

results.seo_title: MedVol-R1: A Reinforcement Learning-Based Framework for Volumetric…
results.meta_description: MedVol-R1 is a reinforcement learning-based framework that outperforms strong baselines in volumetric reasoning segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art…
results.body_html:
MedVol-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines in volumetric reasoning segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

LVLM grounds clinical reasoning to a verifiable 2D evidence anchor (key axial slice and 2D bounding boxes) MedSAM2 module propagates the 2D anchor into a coherent 3D mask Multi-component reward encourages informative evidence selection, accurate 2D spatial grounding, and cross-slice volumetric coherence

CT-ORG, AbdomenCT-1K, and KiTS23 State-of-the-art performance with reinforcement learning providing clear gains over pure supervised fine-tuning

Benchmarks and Gaps Experiments demonstrate that MedVol-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines, but further research is needed to address potential gaps in performance on diverse narrative expressions.

source.title: MedVol-R1: Reward-Driven Evidence Grounding for Volumetric Reasoning Segmentation
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new framework called MedVol-R1 for Volumetric Reasoning Segmentation (VRS), which aims to segment 3D medical scans based on free-form clinical queries. The framework uses reinforcement learning to explicitly decouple evidence grounding from volumetric delineation, allowing for more interpretable and generalizable results. In experiments, MedVol-R1 outperformed strong baselines and achieved state-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of its approach.

results.seo_title: Big Data Analytics for Diabetes Management in Rwanda's Healthcare…
results.meta_description: A study examines the readiness of Rwanda's healthcare system to adopt big data analytics for diabetes management and proposes a practical BDA framework.
results.body_html:
The Rwanda healthcare system is exploring the use of big data analytics to improve diabetes management. This approach has the potential to support early detection and better treatment decisions.

Key FindingsThe study highlights both the potential and main challenges of BDA implementation in Rwanda's healthcare system.A five-day workshop involving 25 key stakeholders was conducted to assess preparedness and identify existing gaps.The findings suggest that new opportunities arise for improving prediction, monitoring, and clinical decision-making as the country expands its use of electronic medical records and health information systems.Proposed BDA FrameworkBased on these results, the paper proposes a practical BDA framework to support diabetes management strategies using explainable machine learning models.Challenges and LimitationsChallengeDescriptionLimited data qualityThe study highlights the need for

high-quality data to support BDA implementation in Rwanda's healthcare system.Lack of standardizationThe findings suggest that standardization of health information systems is necessary to ensure seamless integration with BDA tools.Insufficient stakeholder engagementThe workshop involved 25 key stakeholders, but further engagement is needed to ensure buy-in from all relevant parties.
target_keywords: Big Data Analytics for Diabetes Management

source.title: Implementation of Big Data Analytics for Diabetes Management: Needs Assessment in the Rwanda Healthcare System
source.summary:
Here is a summary of the news item in 2-4 sentences:

A study assessed Rwanda's healthcare system readiness for implementing Big Data Analytics (BDA) for diabetes management. A five-day workshop involving key stakeholders identified both the potential and challenges of BDA implementation. The findings led to the proposal of a practical BDA framework using explainable machine learning models to support diabetes management strategies. This framework aims to improve prediction, monitoring, and clinical decision-making in Rwanda's healthcare system.

results.seo_title: EPPC-OASIS: Ontology-Aware Adaptation for Secure Patient-Provider…
results.meta_description: A new framework for extracting clinically important communication behaviors from secure patient-provider messages.
results.body_html:
The framework achieved 77.13% Code+Sub-code F1 and 63.83% Triplet F1, corresponding to modest but consistent absolute gains over the strongest supervised fine-tuning baseline.

Key Components of EPPC-OASIS

  • EPPC-OASIS augments supervised fine-tuning with a Wasserstein alignment objective that encourages alignment between model representation neighborhoods and EPPC ontology-derived neighborhoods.
  • Inference refinement uses verification, self-consistency, hybrid correction, and selection or ensembling to address residual prediction errors.

Performance Evaluation of EPPC-OASIS

Metric Value
F1 Score (Code+Sub-code) 77.13%
F1 Score (Triplet) 63.83%

Conclusion and Future Work

These results suggest that ontology-aware adaptation with structured inference refinement can support scalable retrospective EPPC mining, although external validation is needed before operational use.

source.title: EPPC-OASIS: Ontology-Aware Adaptation and Structured Inference Refinement for Electronic Patient-Provider Communication Mining in Secure Messages
source.summary: Researchers have developed a framework called EPPC-OASIS to improve the extraction of clinically important communication behaviors from secure patient-provider messages. The framework uses an ontology-aware adaptation approach and structured inference refinement procedures to enhance the accuracy of annotations. In a study, EPPC-OASIS outperformed several baselines across multiple language models, achieving high F1 scores for code/sub-code extraction and triplet extraction. The results suggest that the framework can support scalable retrospective mining of electronic patient-provider communication.

results.seo_title: AI in Healthcare: ConceptM$^3$oE for Interpretable Computational…
results.meta_description: ConceptM$^3$oE: A novel AI framework for interpretable computational pathology, improving performance and interpretability in healthcare.
results.body_html:
What is ConceptM^3oE? ConceptM^3oE is a novel AI framework for interpretable computational pathology that improves performance and interpretability in healthcare.

Improving PerformanceCompetitive performance to unconstrained modelsImproved limited-data performance, increasing macro-F1 from 56.41% to 66.70%Enhancing InterpretabilityKey FeaturesDescriptionConcept formation within interaction-aware mixture-of-experts (MoE) pathwaysEmbeds concept formation directly into the architectureResidual pathways for task-relevant signals to flow through concepts and directly to final task predictionPrevents information loss typical of interpretable bottlenecksValidation and ResultsValidated by an independent neuropathologist, delivering competitive performance in pediatric brain tumor and public glioma cohorts.

source.title: ConceptM$^3$oE: Concept-Guided Multimodal Mixture of Experts for Interpretable Computational Pathology
source.summary: Researchers have developed ConceptM$^3$oE, an architecture for multimodal mixture-of-experts (MoE) models in computational pathology. The model uses concept formation to clarify how diverse diagnostic signals assemble into recognizable concepts, allowing for more interpretable predictions. In experiments on pediatric brain tumor and glioma cohorts, the framework achieved competitive performance with unconstrained models while producing valid reasoning traces, and showed improved limited-data performance and faster training convergence compared to non-concept-informed baselines.

results.seo_title: AI-ECG Predictions Align with Echocardiographic Measures of Heart…
results.meta_description: Researchers investigated the association between AI-ECG predictions and echocardiographic measures of heart failure, finding strong correlations with global…
results.body_html:
Direct answer to the question of whether AI-ECG predictions align with echocardiographic measures of heart failure is yes, according to a recent study.

Global longitudinal strain showed the strongest correlation (ρ=0.57) between AI-ECG risk and echocardiographic parameters. Mitral annular plane systolic excursion (MAPSE) had a moderate correlation (-0.49). LVEF had a weaker correlation (-0.45).

Correlation Coefficients Parameterρ (Men)ρ (Women)LVEF-0.45-0.35Volumetric left ventricular indices-0.25-0.15Diastolic-related parameters0.200.30

Patient Subgroups with Stronger Correlations

Patients with LVEF greater than 50% had substantial correlations for GLS, MAPSE, and diastolic-related parameters.

source.title: Associations between echocardiographic traits and AI-ECG predictions of heart failure
source.summary: Researchers analyzed data from over 8,400 patients to investigate the relationship between AI-ECG predictions of heart failure and echocardiographic measures of myocardial dysfunction. The study found that global longitudinal strain (GLS) showed the strongest correlation with AI-ECG risk, followed by mitral annular plane systolic excursion (MAPSE) and left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF). These findings suggest that AI-ECG models can capture heart failure risk beyond what is detected by traditional measures of LVEF.

results.seo_title: HeartBeatAI: Robust Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Label ECG…
results.meta_description: A deep learning framework combining domain generalization, multi-scale feature aggregation, and clinical explainability for robust 12-lead ECG classification.
results.body_html:
HeartBeatAI achieves a 98% Macro F1-score under intra-source conditions, demonstrating its robustness and performance in 12-lead ECG classification.

Combines domain generalization, multi-scale feature aggregation, and clinical explainability for robust ECG classification Integrates a Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) ResNet to isolate diagnostic leads Multi-Layer Concentration Pipeline captures macro-rhythm and micro-morphological anomalies

LODO Decreased performance in detecting rare anomalies

Challenges and Limitations Significant degradation in detecting rare anomalies highlights a persistent challenge in cross-institutional deployment.

source.title: HeartBeatAI: An Interpretable and Robust Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Label ECG Arrhythmia Detection
source.summary: Researchers have developed HeartBeatAI, a deep learning framework designed to improve the accuracy of electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis for arrhythmia detection. The framework addresses challenges such as class imbalance and generalization gap by incorporating domain generalization, multi-scale feature aggregation, and clinical explainability. It has shown high performance in intra-source evaluations but struggled with detecting rare anomalies in cross-institutional deployments.

results.seo_title: AI for Mental Health Analysis: Exploring Perceptual Speech Features
results.meta_description: Researchers develop a systematic feature-based analysis framework to support mental health assessment through objective and interpretable cues.
results.body_html:
The framework reveals stable and consistent relationships between symptom severity and vocal irregularities, lexical-syntactic patterns, and affective tone. Researchers develop a systematic feature-based analysis framework to support mental health assessment through objective and interpretable cues.

Key Features of the FrameworkPerceptually grounded acoustic and linguistic characteristics, including prosody, vocal quality, semantic coherence, syntactic structure, and sarcasmStatistical analysis and interpretable machine learning (XGBoost with SHAP and LIME)Associations between speech features and validated symptom measures of depression, anxiety, and ADHDDataset EvaluationDatasetControlled Benchmark DatasetsReal-World Clinical DatasetStressIDDAIC-WOZ, Androids, EATDYesDAIC-WOZAndroids, EATDNoAndroidsEATDNoEATDNoNoAblation Study ResultsThe ablation study identifies the most informative feature groups, revealing stable and consistent relationships between symptom severity and vocal irregularities, lexical-syntactic patterns, and affective tone.

source.title: Exploration of Perceptual Speech Features for Clinical Decision-Support in Mental Health Care
source.summary: Researchers have developed a framework for analyzing speech features to support clinical decision-making in mental health care. The framework uses machine learning and statistical analysis to identify associations between speech characteristics, such as prosody and syntax, and validated measures of depression, anxiety, and ADHD. The study found stable relationships between symptom severity and certain vocal irregularities and lexical-syntactic patterns. This approach aims to provide a transparent and clinically interpretable tool for mental health assessment.

results.seo_title: AI-Powered Longitudinal Data Retrieval for Chronic Dermatologic…
results.meta_description: Researchers developed a privacy-preserving local language model to retrieve structured clinical features and generate longitudinal summaries from long-term…
results.body_html:
The developed AI-powered system can retrieve structured clinical features and generate longitudinal summaries from long-term dermatology follow-up records with a mean accuracy of 82.25%.

Mean accuracy: 82.25% Dermatologists' ratings: high for overall quality, clinical accuracy, and usefulness No significant inter-evaluator differences in ratings

Longitudinal Data Retrieval Capabilities

Across 1,680 feature retrieval tasks Mean accuracy: 82.25%

Benefits and Potential Applications SLMs may support clinical decision-making when integrated with appropriate oversight.

source.title: Privacy-Preserving Local Language Models for Longitudinal Data Retrieval in Chronic Dermatologic Disease: Implementation in Pemphigus Patients
source.summary: Researchers have developed a small language model to retrieve and summarize structured clinical features from long-term dermatology follow-up records of pemphigus patients, a chronic skin disease. The locally deployed model achieved high accuracy (82.25%) in retrieving 56 clinically relevant features from 541 visit notes, with dermatologists rating the AI-generated summaries as highly accurate and useful. This study suggests that privacy-preserving language models can outperform medical experts in generating clinically meaningful longitudinal summaries, potentially supporting clinical decision-making. The findings have implications for improving patient care and reducing clinician workload.

results.seo_title: Task-Aligned Self-Supervised Learning for Medical Image Analysis: A…
results.meta_description: A systematic review of self-supervised learning in medical image analysis, providing practical design guidelines and insights into its effectiveness.
results.body_html:
Self-supervised learning (SSL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for addressing the annotation bottleneck in medical imaging by learning representations from unlabeled data.

Contrastive methods learn global discriminative features and align well with classification, but may overlook subtle pathological patterns. Generative and spatial prediction-based approaches better preserve local anatomical structure, making them more suitable for segmentation and other dense prediction tasks. Hybrid methods offer the most balanced performance.

Modality-Specific Design and Performance

Low-label and few-shot regimes SSL provides its greatest benefit.

High-dimensional data Resource-efficient training is critical.

Open Challenges and Future Directions Pathology-aware pretext task design, standardized evaluation protocols, and resource-efficient training for high-dimensional data are key challenges to overcome.

source.title: Task-Aligned Self-Supervised Learning for Medical Image Analysis: A Systematic Review and Practical Design Guidelines
source.summary:
Here is a summary of the news item in 2-4 sentences:

A systematic review of self-supervised learning (SSL) methods in medical image analysis has identified four paradigms: contrastive, non-contrastive and predictive, generative and reconstruction-based, and hybrid learning. The review found that performance depends on the alignment between the pretext task, imaging modality, and target task, with some paradigms better suited for specific tasks such as classification or segmentation. The study also highlights the importance of modality-specific design and provides practical guidelines for designing effective SSL frameworks in medical imaging. The findings aim to provide guidance for researchers and practitioners seeking to develop more clinically relevant SSL methods.

results.seo_title: RAG4Outcome: A Retrieval-Augmented Multimodal Framework for…
results.meta_description: A novel retrieval-augmented generation framework for prognostic prediction in chronic osteomyelitis, integrating multimodal clinical data and expert-guided…
results.body_html:
Chronic osteomyelitis presents substantial prognostic challenges due to its high recurrence risk and complex postoperative recovery trajectories.

MethodologyIntegrates multimodal clinical data, including PET-CT imaging reports, structured surgical and diagnostic records, and unstructured follow-up notes, into a unified prediction pipeline.Combines a domain-specific retrieval corpus with expert-guided prompting to enable more interpretable, evidence-grounded, and clinically reliable prognosis.Preliminary Results Preliminary results on real-world cases demonstrate promising effectiveness and clinical alignment, highlighting the potential of RAG4Outcome for AI-assisted infection management and postoperative decision support. Benchmarks and LimitationsChallengeCurrent StateFuture WorkHeterogeneous clinical dataPresents challenges for current multimodal learning approaches that require aligned inputs and large annotated datasets.RAG4Outcome's domain-specific retrieval corpus and expert-guided prompting

address this challenge.Scalability and efficiencyTraditional assessment often relies on manual scoring systems, which limit scalability, efficiency, and consistency in clinical practice.RAG4Outcome's unified prediction pipeline enables more efficient and scalable prognostic prediction.
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: RAG4Outcome: A Retrieval-Augmented Multimodal Framework for Prognostic Prediction in Chronic Osteomyelitis
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new framework called RAG4Outcome to improve prognostic prediction in chronic osteomyelitis, a condition with high recurrence risk and complex recovery trajectories. The framework combines multimodal clinical data from various sources into a unified pipeline, enabling more interpretable and clinically reliable prognosis. Preliminary results show promising effectiveness of the method, highlighting its potential for AI-assisted infection management and postoperative decision support.
